title: Leadership Rounds
description: AI ROI, stakeholder alignment, adoption, governance, risk, vendor management, team enablement, and business impact.

questions.title: How do you measure ROI for an AI initiative?
questions.scenario: Executives ask whether an internal assistant is worth continued investment.
questions.expectedAnswer: Tie AI work to measurable business outcomes such as time saved, revenue lift, risk reduction, quality improvement, deflection, or faster cycle time. Include adoption, operating cost, accuracy, human review cost, and opportunity cost. Avoid measuring only model accuracy or demo quality.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Leadership Rounds?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise leadership rounds setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you manage stakeholders for GenAI projects?
questions.scenario: A GenAI project touches HR data and customer-facing workflows.
questions.expectedAnswer: Align product, legal, security, compliance, data owners, engineering, and end users early. Define use-case scope, risk level, success metrics, review checkpoints, and escalation paths. Strong leaders communicate uncertainty and tradeoffs clearly.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Leadership Rounds?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise leadership rounds setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you drive adoption of AI tools?
questions.scenario: A well-built assistant has low usage after launch.
questions.expectedAnswer: Adoption requires solving real pain points, workflow integration, trust, training, feedback channels, champions, measurement, and iteration. Users need clear boundaries of when to trust, verify, or escalate AI outputs.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Leadership Rounds?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise leadership rounds setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you manage GenAI risk?
questions.scenario: A business team wants to automate customer-impacting decisions with an LLM.
questions.expectedAnswer: Identify risks such as hallucination, privacy leakage, bias, security, compliance, vendor dependency, and automation errors. Mitigate with governance, human review, access controls, evaluations, logging, red teaming, policy, and incident response.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Leadership Rounds?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise leadership rounds setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you enable teams to build GenAI responsibly?
questions.scenario: Many product teams want to ship AI features but lack deep GenAI experience.
questions.expectedAnswer: Provide reference architectures, shared platforms, approved models, reusable evaluation tools, security patterns, training, documentation, and office hours. Balance centralized governance with team autonomy.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Leadership Rounds?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise leadership rounds setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How would you explain AI portfolio prioritization in a Leadership Rounds interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through AI portfolio prioritization for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define AI portfolio prioritization, explain why it matters for leadership rounds, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
